skill_name: Wishes & Regrets (I wish / If only / Should have)

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: I wish I ___ (know) how to drive — I have to take the bus everywhere and it takes so long.
distractor_rationale: would cannot be used in a wish clause referring to a present state; this is the classic learner error of inserting would
had known is past perfect and expresses regret about a past event, but the context describes a current inability — taking the bus now
the base form know is not a past form and cannot express an unreal present wish

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Tom ___ (call) his grandmother on her birthday last week — she was very upset that he forgot.
distractor_rationale: should call refers to a present or future obligation, not a past failure that already happened last week
should of called is a spelling error based on the spoken contraction; of is never the correct auxiliary here
had called is past perfect and does not carry the meaning of obligation or regret about a past action

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: If only the weather ___ (be) warmer right now — we could sit outside and enjoy the garden.
distractor_rationale: would be cannot follow if only in a present wish clause; would is reserved for the result clause
had been is past perfect and signals regret about a past situation, but right now makes clear this is a current wish
is is the indicative present form and cannot express an unreal or wished-for condition

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: I wish I ___ (take) that job offer in the city two years ago — my career would be so different now.
distractor_rationale: took is past simple and expresses an unreal present wish, but the time marker two years ago and the result clause show this is a regret about a completed past decision
would have taken is the error of placing would in the wish clause; would belongs in the result clause, not after wish
have taken is present perfect and cannot express an unreal past regret in a wish clause

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Anna ___ (save) more money before she moved into her own flat — she is struggling to pay the bills every month.
distractor_rationale: should save expresses a present or future recommendation, not a regret about a completed past action before she moved
could save expresses present ability or possibility, not a past obligation that was not fulfilled
should had saved is an incorrect construction; the correct form after should is have, not had

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: If only I ___ (have) more free time these days — there are so many hobbies I want to try.
distractor_rationale: would have cannot appear in the if only clause; would is used in the result clause of a conditional, not in the wish itself
had had is past perfect and expresses a regret about the past, but these days makes clear the wish is about the present situation
have is the indicative present form and cannot express an unreal or wished-for present state

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: We ___ (leave) the house earlier this morning — we missed the first train and had to wait an hour for the next one.
distractor_rationale: could leave refers to a present or future possibility, not a missed opportunity that occurred this morning in the past
should of left is a spelling error based on the spoken contraction; of is never the correct auxiliary after a modal
could had left is an incorrect construction; the correct form after a modal is have, not had

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: If only I ___ (listen) to my doctor's advice last year and changed my diet — I might not be dealing with these health problems now.
distractor_rationale: listened is past simple and expresses an unreal present wish; the time marker last year and the result clause about current health problems confirm this is a past regret requiring past perfect
would have listened incorrectly places would in the if only clause; would belongs in the result clause, not after if only
have listened is present perfect and cannot function as an unreal past form inside a wish or if only clause

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: I wish I ___ (speak) Spanish fluently — I would feel so much more confident during the meetings with our colleagues from abroad.
distractor_rationale: had spoken is past perfect and signals regret about a past event, but the result clause about feeling confident during current meetings confirms this is an unreal present wish
would speak is the common error of using would in the wish clause; would cannot follow wish when expressing a present unreal state
speak is the base form and cannot express the unreal or hypothetical meaning required after I wish

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: David ___ (book) the hotel room in advance when he had the chance — now everything is full and he has nowhere to stay.
distractor_rationale: should book expresses a present or future recommendation, not a regret about a past opportunity that was missed when he had the chance
would have booked is the result form of a third conditional and expresses a hypothetical past outcome, not a criticism or regret about an obligation
should have been booking uses past perfect continuous after should have, which is grammatically incorrect; the simple infinitive is required after should have

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Mark ___ have called the hotel before leaving home — there were no rooms available when he arrived.
distractor_rationale: 'should of' is a phonetic spelling error; the correct auxiliary is 'should have', not 'should of'
'would have called' expresses a hypothetical result, not a past regret or criticism about failing to do something
'must have called' expresses deduction about what happened, not regret that something was not done

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: If only she ___ (accept) the job offer last spring — the salary was much better than what she earns now.
distractor_rationale: past simple 'accepted' after 'if only' refers to an unreal present wish, but the time marker 'last spring' and the comparison to current earnings confirm this is a past regret requiring the past perfect
'would have accepted' is a common learner error placing 'would' in the wish clause; 'would' belongs in the result clause of a conditional, not after 'if only'
present perfect 'has accepted' cannot express an unreal past regret; the past perfect is required for wishes about completed past events

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: I wish I ___ (live) closer to the city centre — the commute every morning takes almost two hours.
distractor_rationale: 'would live' in the wish clause is a typical learner error; 'would' is not used after 'wish' to express a present unreal wish
'had lived' signals a past regret, but the present-tense context ('the commute every morning takes') confirms this is a wish about the current situation
present continuous 'am living' cannot follow 'wish' to express an unreal present situation; the past simple is required

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: If only we ___ (book) the tickets earlier — by the time we checked online, the concert was completely sold out.
distractor_rationale: past simple 'booked' after 'if only' signals a present unreal wish, but the past time sequence ('by the time we checked') establishes that the regret concerns a completed past action, requiring the past perfect
'could book' is a present or future modal form and cannot express regret about a specific past event that has already concluded
'would have booked' places 'would' inside the 'if only' clause, which is a common learner error; 'would' belongs in the result clause of a conditional, never in the wish or condition clause

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: I wish I ___ how to drive — it would make getting to work so much easier.
distractor_rationale: using 'would' in the wish clause is a classic learner error; wish + would is only used for annoyance about someone else's behaviour, not for personal ability
present simple 'know' cannot express an unreal present wish; past simple is required after 'I wish' for present unreality
'had known' is past perfect, used for regret about a past situation, but the context describes a current wish about a present ability

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: She ___ a warmer coat before she left for the mountains last weekend.
distractor_rationale: 'should pack' is a present/future obligation form and cannot express regret about a completed past action
'should of' is a phonetic spelling error; the correct auxiliary is 'have', not 'of'
'should has' is grammatically impossible; the auxiliary after 'should' is always the base form 'have', not 'has'

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: If only I ___ more time to spend with my family every evening.
distractor_rationale: present simple 'have' cannot express an unreal present wish; past simple is required after 'if only' for present unreality
'would have' in the if-only clause is incorrect; 'would' is not used in the wish/if-only clause itself for present wishes
'had had' is past perfect, appropriate for regret about a past situation, but the context ('every evening') clearly describes a present recurring wish

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: Tom wishes he ___ the job offer from the city firm three years ago — he regrets turning it down.
distractor_rationale: past simple 'accepted' cannot express an unreal past regret after 'wishes'; past perfect is required for past wishes
'would accept' is incorrect in the wish clause; 'would' cannot be used to express the speaker's own unreal past wish
present perfect 'has accepted' describes a real completed action, not an unreal past regret; past perfect is required after 'wishes' for past unreality

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: If only we ___ the restaurant in advance — there were no tables left when we arrived.
distractor_rationale: past simple 'booked' cannot follow 'if only' to express a past regret; past perfect is required to describe an unreal past situation
'would have booked' belongs in the result clause of a third conditional, not in the if-only clause expressing the regret itself
present perfect 'have booked' refers to a real action connected to the present, not an unreal past regret; past perfect is required after 'if only'

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: Mark ___ his sister's birthday — he completely forgot and she was very upset.
distractor_rationale: 'could have forgotten' expresses an unrealised past possibility but not regret or criticism about a failure; it contradicts the context since he did forget
'should remember' is a present obligation form and cannot express regret or criticism about a past completed failure
'would have remembered' expresses a hypothetical result in a third conditional, not a direct expression of regret or criticism about a past duty

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: Anna wishes she ___ closer to her workplace so that she didn't have to travel for two hours every day.
distractor_rationale: 'would live' is incorrect in the wish clause for a personal present wish; 'would' is reserved for expressing annoyance about another person's behaviour
'had lived' is past perfect, used to express regret about a past situation, but the time clue 'every day' makes clear this is a present ongoing wish
present simple 'lives' cannot express an unreal present wish; past simple is required after 'wishes' to signal present unreality

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: If only David ___ the warning signs earlier — the project might not have failed so badly.
distractor_rationale: past simple 'noticed' cannot follow 'if only' to express an unreal past regret; past perfect is required to describe what did not happen before a past result
'would have noticed' belongs in the hypothetical result clause, not in the if-only clause that expresses the regret itself
present perfect 'has noticed' describes a real action with present relevance, not an unreal past regret; past perfect is required after 'if only' for past unreality

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: Lisa ___ her presentation more carefully before the meeting — the manager pointed out several mistakes.
distractor_rationale: 'should check' is a present/future recommendation and cannot express an unrealised past possibility or mild criticism about something before a past event
'could have been checking' is past perfect continuous, which emphasises an ongoing action in progress, not a single completed opportunity that was missed
'would check' is a conditional or habitual form and cannot express an unrealised past possibility; it does not convey the sense of a missed opportunity before the meeting

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: I wish I ___ so much money on things I don't really need — my savings account is almost empty now.
distractor_rationale: past simple 'didn't spend' cannot follow 'I wish' to express regret about a completed past action; past perfect is required to signal that the spending happened before now and cannot be undone
'wouldn't spend' is incorrect in the wish clause for expressing personal past regret; 'would' in wish clauses is used for annoyance about another person's present habits, not one's own past actions
present perfect 'haven't spent' describes a real situation up to now, not an unreal past regret; past perfect is required after 'I wish' to express regret about something that did happen

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: I wish I would know the answer to that question right now.
content.error_sentence: I wish I would know the answer to that question right now.
content.corrected_sentence: I wish I knew the answer to that question right now.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: She should of called the doctor when she first felt ill yesterday morning.
content.error_sentence: She should of called the doctor when she first felt ill yesterday morning.
content.corrected_sentence: She should have called the doctor when she first felt ill yesterday morning.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: I wish I had more free time last weekend to spend with my family.
content.error_sentence: I wish I had more free time last weekend to spend with my family.
content.corrected_sentence: I wish I had had more free time last weekend to spend with my family.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: If only he would listen to his teacher's advice during the exam preparation.
content.error_sentence: If only he would listen to his teacher's advice during the exam preparation.
content.corrected_sentence: If only he listened to his teacher's advice during the exam preparation.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: Tom wishes he studied harder before his driving test last month.
content.error_sentence: Tom wishes he studied harder before his driving test last month.
content.corrected_sentence: Tom wishes he had studied harder before his driving test last month.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: We could of arrived at the station on time if we had left the house earlier.
content.error_sentence: We could of arrived at the station on time if we had left the house earlier.
content.corrected_sentence: We could have arrived at the station on time if we had left the house earlier.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: If only Anna would have told us about the change of plan before we booked the tickets.
content.error_sentence: If only Anna would have told us about the change of plan before we booked the tickets.
content.corrected_sentence: If only Anna had told us about the change of plan before we booked the tickets.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: I wish I would be more confident when I speak in front of a large group of people.
content.error_sentence: I wish I would be more confident when I speak in front of a large group of people.
content.corrected_sentence: I wish I were more confident when I speak in front of a large group of people.

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: I wish I ___ (know) how to drive — I have to take the bus everywhere and it takes so long.
distractor_rationale: using 'would' in a wish clause is a classic learner error; 'would' is never used in the clause after 'wish' for present wishes
'had known' expresses regret about a past situation, but the context ('I have to take the bus') shows this is a present wish about a current inability
present simple 'know' cannot express an unreal present wish; the past simple is required to signal unreality

duplicate: exact duplicate of a prior item

rejected.rejection_reason: duplicate: exact duplicate of a prior item
